- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""I-deepfix-001 fix I5 — DeepTRACE benchmark-judge provider/headroom preflight (the measurement gate).
3	
4	WHY THIS EXISTS (the measurement precondition, closes the Codex iter-2 novel P1)
5	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6	The DeepTRACE 8-metric scorer (``scripts/dr_benchmark/deeptrace_scorer.py``) needs a binary
7	Factual-Support Matrix ``S[i][j] = "does listed source j support statement i?"``. That per-(statement,
8	source) decision is made by a BENCHMARK-SCORER judge locked SEPARATELY from the runtime faithfulness
9	judge in ``config/benchmark/deeptrace_judge_lock.yaml``. A drb_72-class report makes ~178 statement-
10	support calls under concurrency, so the locked judge MUST have real OpenRouter provider redundancy — a
11	low-provider model 429s and the support matrix comes back PARTIAL, which silently produces a WRONG
12	DeepTRACE number. And a number scored under an UNSIGNED lock is not an operator-authorized measurement.
13	
14	THIS PREFLIGHT FAILS LOUD (refuses to let any DeepTRACE number be claimed) when ANY of:
15	  1. the lock is UNSIGNED (``signature.signed`` is not true) — the lock is inert until the operator signs.
16	  2. the locked benchmark judge has FEWER operational OpenRouter providers than
17	     ``provider_headroom.min_operational_provider_count`` — the support matrix would return partial.
18	  3. provider headroom could NOT be measured (no ``OPENROUTER_API_KEY`` / network error) — never a
19	     silent pass; an unmeasured headroom is a blocker, not an OK.
20	  4. the comparability policy is missing or not one of the recognized honest paths — a number produced
21	     under an undeclared comparability policy is not comparable and must not be claimed.
22	
23	DNA: this is a MEASUREMENT gate + measurement-judge lock, not a pipeline knob. It has NO cap / target /
24	thinner on the pipeline; it only decides whether a DeepTRACE NUMBER is claimable. Fail-loud, never a
25	silent fallback (LAW II). Every threshold comes from the operator-signed lock (LAW VI).
26	
27	BUILD-ONLY / OFFLINE-TESTABLE
28	-----------------------------
29	``evaluate_preflight(lock, operational_provider_count)`` is a PURE function over a lock dict + an integer
30	count (offline, $0, no network) — that is what the unit test exercises RED->GREEN. Only ``--check`` touches
31	the network (a single GET to OpenRouter ``/models/{slug}/endpoints`` to count operational providers, using
32	the SAME operational definition as ``scripts/diagnostics/rank_openrouter_providers.py``: ``status == 0``
33	AND ``uptime_last_30m >= PG_PROVIDER_UPTIME_FLOOR``).
34	
35	Usage
36	-----
37	    # offline: evaluate the lock with a known provider count (e.g. from a prior --check)
38	    python scripts/dr_benchmark/deeptrace_judge_preflight.py --provider-count 21
39	
40	    # live: count operational providers for the locked judge, then evaluate (exit 0 clean, 4 == blocked)
41	    python scripts/dr_benchmark/deeptrace_judge_preflight.py --check
42	"""
43	from __future__ import annotations
44	
45	import argparse
46	import json
47	import os
48	import sys
49	import urllib.request
50	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
51	from typing import Any, Mapping, Optional
52	
53	# The default DeepTRACE benchmark-judge lock path (LAW VI: overridable via --lock).
54	DEFAULT_LOCK_PATH = "config/benchmark/deeptrace_judge_lock.yaml"
55	
56	# Operational-endpoint definition, IDENTICAL to scripts/diagnostics/rank_openrouter_providers.py so the
57	# headroom count matches the routing layer's notion of "healthy". LAW VI: env-overridable floor.
58	_UPTIME_FLOOR = float(os.getenv("PG_PROVIDER_UPTIME_FLOOR", "98.0"))
59	
60	# The honest comparability policies the lock may declare (arXiv 2509.04499 measurement discipline).
61	_VALID_COMPARABILITY_POLICIES = {"paper_judge", "self_rescore"}
62	
63	
64	@dataclass
65	class PreflightResult:
66	    """The outcome of the DeepTRACE benchmark-judge preflight. ``claimable`` is True IFF a DeepTRACE
67	    number may be claimed (lock signed, provider headroom met + measured, comparability declared)."""
68	
69	    claimable: bool
70	    blockers: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
71	    signed: bool = False
72	    model_slug: Optional[str] = None
73	    min_provider_floor: Optional[int] = None
74	    operational_provider_count: Optional[int] = None
75	    comparability_policy: Optional[str] = None
76	
77	
78	def load_lock(path: str = DEFAULT_LOCK_PATH) -> dict:
79	    """Load the benchmark-judge lock YAML. Raises FileNotFoundError (fail-loud) if it is missing — a
80	    DeepTRACE number cannot be claimed without the operator-signed lock present."""
81	    import yaml  # local import: keep the module import lightweight for the pure path
82	
83	    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
84	        data = yaml.safe_load(fh)
85	    if not isinstance(data, dict):
86	        raise ValueError(f"{path}: expected a YAML mapping, got {type(data).__name__}")
87	    return data
88	
89	
90	def _as_bool(value: Any) -> bool:
91	    if isinstance(value, bool):
92	        return value
93	    return str(value).strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
94	
95	
96	def evaluate_preflight(
97	    lock: Mapping[str, Any],
98	    operational_provider_count: Optional[int],
99	) -> PreflightResult:
100	    """PURE preflight (offline, $0). Decide whether a DeepTRACE number is claimable for the given lock +
101	    measured operational provider count. ``operational_provider_count is None`` means "could not measure"
102	    and is itself a blocker (never a silent pass).
103	
104	    Blockers (fail-loud, LAW II):
105	      * lock unsigned;
106	      * comparability policy missing / unrecognized;
107	      * provider headroom unmeasured (count is None);
108	      * operational provider count below the lock's ``min_operational_provider_count`` floor.
109	    """
110	    blockers: list[str] = []
111	
112	    signature = lock.get("signature") or {}
113	    signed = _as_bool(signature.get("signed", False))
114	    if not signed:
115	        blockers.append(
116	            "benchmark-judge lock is UNSIGNED (signature.signed is not true) — the lock is INERT until "
117	            "an operator signs it; NO DeepTRACE number is claimable. Sign "
118	            f"{DEFAULT_LOCK_PATH} (signature.signed:true + operator + signed_commit)."
119	        )
120	
121	    benchmark_judge = lock.get("benchmark_judge") or {}
122	    model_slug = benchmark_judge.get("model_slug")
123	    if not model_slug:
124	        blockers.append("benchmark_judge.model_slug is empty — the benchmark judge is not declared.")
125	
126	    comparability = lock.get("comparability") or {}
127	    policy = comparability.get("policy")
128	    if policy not in _VALID_COMPARABILITY_POLICIES:
129	        blockers.append(
130	            f"comparability.policy={policy!r} is not one of {sorted(_VALID_COMPARABILITY_POLICIES)} — a "
131	            "DeepTRACE number produced under an undeclared comparability policy is not comparable and "
132	            "must not be claimed (never mix self_rescore POLARIS numbers with paper-published numbers)."
133	        )
134	
135	    headroom = lock.get("provider_headroom") or {}
136	    try:
137	        min_floor = int(headroom.get("min_operational_provider_count"))
138	    except (TypeError, ValueError):
139	        min_floor = None
140	    if min_floor is None:
141	        blockers.append(
142	            "provider_headroom.min_operational_provider_count is missing/invalid — the headroom floor "
143	            "is undefined, so headroom cannot be enforced."
144	        )
145	
146	    if operational_provider_count is None:
147	        blockers.append(
148	            "provider headroom could NOT be measured (no OPENROUTER_API_KEY or a network error on the "
149	            "/models/{slug}/endpoints probe) — an UNMEASURED headroom is a blocker, never a silent pass. "
150	            "Run --check with OPENROUTER_API_KEY set."
151	        )
152	    elif min_floor is not None and operational_provider_count < min_floor:
153	        blockers.append(
154	            f"benchmark judge {model_slug!r} has {operational_provider_count} operational OpenRouter "
155	            f"provider(s) < the lock floor of {min_floor} — the ~"
156	            f"{headroom.get('expected_statement_support_calls_per_report', '?')} statement-support "
157	            "calls/report would 429 and the Factual-Support Matrix would return PARTIAL -> a wrong "
158	            "DeepTRACE number. Raise headroom (a higher-provider judge) or lower the operator-signed "
159	            "floor before claiming a number."
160	        )
161	
162	    return PreflightResult(
163	        claimable=not blockers,
164	        blockers=blockers,
165	        signed=signed,
166	        model_slug=model_slug,
167	        min_provider_floor=min_floor,
168	        operational_provider_count=operational_provider_count,
169	        comparability_policy=policy,
170	    )
171	
172	
173	def count_operational_providers(model_slug: str, timeout: float = 30.0) -> Optional[int]:
174	    """Count OpenRouter endpoints for ``model_slug`` that are OPERATIONAL (status == 0 AND
175	    uptime_last_30m >= PG_PROVIDER_UPTIME_FLOOR). Returns None on any network/auth failure (the caller
176	    treats None as a fail-loud blocker — never a silent 0-or-pass). Network; only called by --check."""
177	    api_key = os.environ.get("OPENROUTER_API_KEY", "").strip()
178	    if not api_key:
179	        return None
180	    url = f"https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models/{model_slug}/endpoints"
181	    req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"})
182	    try:
183	        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=timeout) as resp:
184	            payload = json.loads(resp.read())
185	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — any probe failure => unmeasured (None), a fail-loud blocker upstream
186	        return None
187	    endpoints = (payload.get("data") or {}).get("endpoints") or []
188	    operational = 0
189	    for ep in endpoints:
190	        up = ep.get("uptime_last_30m")
191	        if ep.get("status") == 0 and (up is None or up >= _UPTIME_FLOOR):
192	            operational += 1
193	    return operational
194	
195	
196	def _print_result(result: PreflightResult) -> None:
197	    print("[deeptrace-judge-preflight] benchmark judge:", result.model_slug)
198	    print(f"[deeptrace-judge-preflight] lock signed: {result.signed}")
199	    print(
200	        "[deeptrace-judge-preflight] operational providers:",
201	        result.operational_provider_count,
202	        f"(floor {result.min_provider_floor})",
203	    )
204	    print("[deeptrace-judge-preflight] comparability policy:", result.comparability_policy)
205	    if result.claimable:
206	        print("[deeptrace-judge-preflight] OK — a DeepTRACE number is claimable under this lock.")
207	    else:
208	        print("[deeptrace-judge-preflight] BLOCKED — a DeepTRACE number is NOT claimable:")
209	        for b in result.blockers:
210	            print(f"  - {b}")
211	
212	
213	def main(argv: Optional[list[str]] = None) -> int:
214	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(
215	        description="DeepTRACE benchmark-judge provider/headroom preflight (I5 measurement gate)."
216	    )
217	    ap.add_argument("--lock", default=DEFAULT_LOCK_PATH, help="path to the benchmark-judge lock YAML")
218	    ap.add_argument(
219	        "--check",
220	        action="store_true",
221	        help="LIVE: count operational OpenRouter providers for the locked judge, then evaluate",
222	    )
223	    ap.add_argument(
224	        "--provider-count",
225	        type=int,
226	        default=None,
227	        help="offline: evaluate against a KNOWN operational-provider count (skips the network probe)",
228	    )
229	    args = ap.parse_args(argv)
230	
231	    try:
232	        lock = load_lock(args.lock)
233	    except (FileNotFoundError, ValueError) as exc:
234	        print(f"[deeptrace-judge-preflight] BLOCKED — cannot load lock: {exc}", file=sys.stderr)
235	        return 4
236	
237	    count: Optional[int] = args.provider_count
238	    if args.check:
239	        model_slug = (lock.get("benchmark_judge") or {}).get("model_slug") or ""
240	        count = count_operational_providers(model_slug) if model_slug else None
241	
242	    result = evaluate_preflight(lock, count)
243	    _print_result(result)
244	    return 0 if result.claimable else 4
245	
246	
247	if __name__ == "__main__":
248	    sys.exit(main())
249

1	"""I-deepfix-001 WS-14 — DeepTRACE citation-faithfulness scorer (re-implementation).
2	
3	DeepTRACE (Salesforce AI Research, arXiv 2509.04499, ICLR 2026) defines 8 audit metrics over a report's
4	statements x sources. There is NO public official scorer/leaderboard, so this is a RE-IMPLEMENTATION whose
5	output is an ESTIMATE, not a proven score: the paper's judge is GPT-5 (Pearson 0.62 vs human); we substitute
6	our D8 kimi-k2.6 judge to build the support matrix (DISCLOSED). Calibrate against the paper's published
7	GPT-5-Deep-Research column before claiming a rank.
8	
9	Inputs (built upstream from the rendered report):
10	  * citation_matrix  C : list[list[int]] of shape [n_statements][n_sources]; C[i][j]=1 iff statement i
11	    cites source j.
12	  * support_matrix   S : list[list[int]] of the same shape; S[i][j]=1 iff source j's content SUPPORTS
13	    statement i (per the judge).
14	  * per-statement metadata: relevant (bool), confidence (int 1-5 or None), stance ('pro'|'con'|'neutral').
15	
16	Exact formulas (paper section on metrics):
17	  III Relevant Statements   = relevant / total
18	  IV  Uncited Sources       = cited / listed           (we also report uncited = 1 - cited/listed)
19	  V   Unsupported Statements = unsupported / relevant   (a relevant statement with no supporting source)
20	  VII Citation Accuracy     = sum(C (x) S) / sum(C)
21	  VIII Citation Thoroughness = sum(C (x) S) / sum(S)
22	  I   One-Sided  (debate)   = 1 iff NOT (>=1 pro AND >=1 con) relevant statement
23	  II  Overconfident (debate)= 1 iff One-Sided AND answer confidence == 5
24	  VI  Source Necessity      = size_of_minimum_source_cover / listed, where the minimum source cover is the
25	      FEWEST sources whose union still SUPPORTS every supported relevant statement (paper 2509.04499
26	      "minimum vertex cover for source nodes"; the official answer-engine-eval reference uses greedy set
27	      cover, which we match — no exact Hopcroft-Karp needed). A statement supported by 2 sources therefore
28	      contributes cover-size 1 (necessity 0.5 of 2 listed), NOT 0. The old SOLE-supporter count (a source
29	      that is the only supporter of some supported relevant statement) is retained as a SECONDARY
30	      disclosure field ``n_sole_supporter`` — it is a lower bound on the cover size, nothing is lost.
31	"""
32	from __future__ import annotations
33	
34	from typing import Any, Optional, Sequence
35	
36	
37	def _sum_hadamard(a: Sequence[Sequence[int]], b: Sequence[Sequence[int]]) -> int:
38	    """sum(a (x) b) — element-wise product summed over the whole matrix."""
39	    total = 0
40	    for row_a, row_b in zip(a, b):
41	        for x, y in zip(row_a, row_b):
42	            total += int(bool(x)) * int(bool(y))
43	    return total
44	
45	
46	def _sum_matrix(a: Sequence[Sequence[int]]) -> int:
47	    return sum(int(bool(x)) for row in a for x in row)
48	
49	
50	def _cited_source_columns(citation_matrix: Sequence[Sequence[int]], n_sources: int) -> set[int]:
51	    cited: set[int] = set()
52	    for row in citation_matrix:
53	        for j in range(min(len(row), n_sources)):
54	            if row[j]:
55	                cited.add(j)
56	    return cited
57	
58	
59	def necessary_source_count(
60	    support_matrix: Sequence[Sequence[int]],
61	    relevant: Sequence[bool],
62	    n_sources: int,
63	) -> int:
64	    """SECONDARY disclosure (NOT the DeepTRACE VI numerator anymore): count sources that are the SOLE
65	    supporter of at least one relevant statement. Such a source must appear in every cover, so this count
66	    is a LOWER BOUND on the minimum source cover size (see ``minimum_source_cover_size``). Redundantly-
67	    supported statements contribute no sole supporter; a statement supported by exactly one source makes
68	    that source a sole supporter. Retained so nothing is lost after the min-cover fix."""
69	    necessary: set[int] = set()
70	    for i, row in enumerate(support_matrix):
71	        if i >= len(relevant) or not relevant[i]:
72	            continue
73	        supporters = [j for j in range(min(len(row), n_sources)) if row[j]]
74	        if len(supporters) == 1:
75	            necessary.add(supporters[0])
76	    return len(necessary)
77	
78	
79	def minimum_source_cover_size(
80	    support_matrix: Sequence[Sequence[int]],
81	    relevant: Sequence[bool],
82	    n_sources: int,
83	) -> int:
84	    """DeepTRACE VI numerator: the size of the MINIMUM source cover over the supported relevant statements.
85	
86	    universe = indices of relevant statements that have >= 1 supporting source (i.e. supported).
87	    sets     = for each listed source j, the set of supported relevant statements it supports (S[i][j]==1).
88	    The cover = the fewest sources whose union covers the whole universe. Computed by greedy set cover
89	    (repeatedly take the source covering the most still-uncovered statements), which is the standard
90	    reference implementation used by the official answer-engine-eval code — exact Hopcroft-Karp is not
91	    required. Deterministic tie-break: lowest source index. A statement supported by 2 sources yields a
92	    cover of size 1 (not 0), fixing the sole-supporter divergence. Returns 0 for an empty universe."""
93	    universe: set[int] = set()
94	    source_sets: dict[int, set[int]] = {j: set() for j in range(n_sources)}
95	    for i, row in enumerate(support_matrix):
96	        if i >= len(relevant) or not relevant[i]:
97	            continue
98	        supporters = [j for j in range(min(len(row), n_sources)) if row[j]]
99	        if not supporters:  # unsupported relevant statement is not in the cover universe
100	            continue
101	        universe.add(i)
102	        for j in supporters:
103	            source_sets[j].add(i)
104	
105	    uncovered = set(universe)
106	    cover_size = 0
107	    while uncovered:
108	        best_j: Optional[int] = None
109	        best_cov = 0
110	        for j in range(n_sources):
111	            cov = len(source_sets[j] & uncovered)
112	            if cov > best_cov:
113	                best_cov = cov
114	                best_j = j
115	        if best_j is None:  # no source covers a remaining statement (unreachable: each has a supporter)
116	            break
117	        uncovered -= source_sets[best_j]
118	        cover_size += 1
119	    return cover_size
120	
121	
122	def _confidence_is_overconfident(
123	    answer_confidence: Optional[int],
124	    statement_confidence: Sequence[Optional[int]],
125	) -> bool:
126	    """The answer is confidence-5 if an explicit answer confidence is 5, else if the max per-statement
127	    confidence is 5 (the paper's confidence scoring is statement-level)."""
128	    if answer_confidence is not None:
129	        return int(answer_confidence) == 5
130	    vals = [int(c) for c in statement_confidence if c is not None]
131	    return bool(vals) and max(vals) == 5
132	
133	
134	def compute_deeptrace_metrics(
135	    *,
136	    citation_matrix: Sequence[Sequence[int]],
137	    support_matrix: Sequence[Sequence[int]],
138	    relevant: Sequence[bool],
139	    n_sources: int,
140	    stance: Optional[Sequence[str]] = None,
141	    statement_confidence: Optional[Sequence[Optional[int]]] = None,
142	    answer_confidence: Optional[int] = None,
143	    is_debate: bool = False,
144	) -> dict[str, Any]:
145	    """Compute all 8 DeepTRACE metrics. Debate-only metrics (One-Sided, Overconfident) are None when
146	    ``is_debate`` is False. Ratios are 0.0 when their denominator is 0 (an empty answer). PURE / offline."""
147	    n_statements = len(citation_matrix)
148	    total = n_statements
149	    n_relevant = sum(1 for i in range(total) if i < len(relevant) and relevant[i])
150	
151	    # III Relevant Statements
152	    relevant_ratio = (n_relevant / total) if total else 0.0
153	
154	    # IV Uncited Sources (report cited fraction + its complement)
155	    cited_cols = _cited_source_columns(citation_matrix, n_sources)
156	    cited_fraction = (len(cited_cols) / n_sources) if n_sources else 0.0
157	    uncited_fraction = (1.0 - cited_fraction) if n_sources else 0.0
158	
159	    # V Unsupported Statements (over RELEVANT statements)
160	    unsupported = 0
161	    for i in range(total):
162	        if i < len(relevant) and relevant[i]:
163	            row = support_matrix[i] if i < len(support_matrix) else []
164	            if not any(row[j] for j in range(min(len(row), n_sources))):
165	                unsupported += 1
166	    unsupported_ratio = (unsupported / n_relevant) if n_relevant else 0.0
167	
168	    # VII / VIII Citation Accuracy / Thoroughness
169	    cs = _sum_hadamard(citation_matrix, support_matrix)
170	    sum_c = _sum_matrix(citation_matrix)
171	    sum_s = _sum_matrix(support_matrix)
172	    citation_accuracy = (cs / sum_c) if sum_c else 0.0
173	    citation_thoroughness = (cs / sum_s) if sum_s else 0.0
174	
175	    # VI Source Necessity = min-source-cover size / listed (paper "minimum vertex cover for source nodes").
176	    cover_size = minimum_source_cover_size(support_matrix, relevant, n_sources)
177	    source_necessity = (cover_size / n_sources) if n_sources else 0.0
178	    # SECONDARY disclosure retained so nothing is lost: the old sole-supporter count (lower bound on cover).
179	    n_sole_supporter = necessary_source_count(support_matrix, relevant, n_sources)
180	
181	    # I / II debate-only
182	    one_sided: Optional[int] = None
183	    overconfident: Optional[int] = None
184	    if is_debate:
185	        st = list(stance or [])
186	        has_pro = any(
187	            (i < len(st) and st[i] == "pro") and (i < len(relevant) and relevant[i])
188	            for i in range(total)
189	        )
190	        has_con = any(
191	            (i < len(st) and st[i] == "con") and (i < len(relevant) and relevant[i])
192	            for i in range(total)
193	        )
194	        one_sided = 0 if (has_pro and has_con) else 1
195	        conf5 = _confidence_is_overconfident(
196	            answer_confidence, list(statement_confidence or [None] * total)
197	        )
198	        overconfident = 1 if (one_sided == 1 and conf5) else 0
199	
200	    return {
201	        "n_statements": total,
202	        "n_relevant_statements": n_relevant,
203	        "n_listed_sources": n_sources,
204	        # lower-better
205	        "one_sided": one_sided,
206	        "overconfident": overconfident,
207	        "uncited_sources_fraction": round(uncited_fraction, 4),
208	        "unsupported_statements_ratio": round(unsupported_ratio, 4),
209	        # higher-better
210	        "relevant_statements_ratio": round(relevant_ratio, 4),
211	        "source_necessity": round(source_necessity, 4),
212	        "source_necessity_cover_size": cover_size,
213	        "n_sole_supporter": n_sole_supporter,
214	        "citation_accuracy": round(citation_accuracy, 4),
215	        "citation_thoroughness": round(citation_thoroughness, 4),
216	        # provenance / honesty
217	        "scorer": "polaris-reimpl-of-deeptrace",
218	        "is_estimate": True,
219	        "judge_substitution": "kimi-k2.6 (paper uses GPT-5) — DISCLOSED",
220	        "source_necessity_interpretation": (
221	            "min-source-cover (greedy set cover over supported relevant statements) / n_listed_sources; "
222	            "n_sole_supporter is the retained sole-supporter lower bound"
223	        ),
224	    }

1	# DeepTRACE BENCHMARK-SCORER judge lock — v1
2	# I-deepfix-001 (GH #1344) WS-14 / fix I5 — the measurement-precondition judge lock.
3	#
4	# WHAT THIS IS
5	# ------------
6	# The DeepTRACE 8-metric scorer (scripts/dr_benchmark/deeptrace_scorer.py) builds a binary
7	# Factual-Support Matrix S[i][j] = "does listed source j support statement i?". That per-(statement,
8	# source) support DECISION is made by a JUDGE. This file locks WHICH judge makes that BENCHMARK
9	# decision, and under WHAT comparability policy a "#1 on DeepTRACE" claim is allowed.
10	#
11	# WHY IT IS SEPARATE FROM THE RUNTIME LOCK (closes Codex iter-2 novel P1)
12	# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
13	# This BENCHMARK-scorer judge is a DISTINCT role from the RUNTIME faithfulness-gating judge in
14	# config/architecture/polaris_runtime_lock.yaml (currently qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b, chosen for runtime
15	# stability + two-family segregation + sovereignty — NOT the properties a COMPARABLE benchmark number
16	# needs). Benchmark scoring is measurement, not runtime gating. Locking them separately keeps the
17	# runtime pipeline's model choice from silently deciding how a leaderboard number is produced.
18	#
19	# MUTATION / SIGNATURE POLICY (LAW VI config; operator is the only signer)
20	# ------------------------------------------------------------------------
21	#   - This lock is INERT until an operator signs it (signature.signed: true + operator + commit).
22	#   - scripts/dr_benchmark/deeptrace_judge_preflight.py FAILS LOUD (refuses to let any DeepTRACE
23	#     number be claimed) while this lock is unsigned, or while the locked judge lacks OpenRouter
24	#     provider headroom for the per-report statement-support call volume.
25	#   - Editing the model, comparability policy, or headroom floor requires a fresh operator signature.
26	
27	version: 1
28	schema_version: 1.0
29	
30	purpose: |
31	  Operator-signed benchmark-scorer judge lock for the DeepTRACE re-implementation. Distinct from the
32	  runtime faithfulness-gating judge lock. Gates whether a DeepTRACE number is claimable at all.
33	
34	distinct_from_runtime_lock: true
35	runtime_lock_ref: config/architecture/polaris_runtime_lock.yaml
36	
37	# === the benchmark-scorer judge ===
38	# Recommended default is a HIGH-provider-availability open reasoner (availability is binding: the
39	# statement-support judge runs ~one call PER (statement, listed-source) support cell, so many calls
40	# per report — a low-provider model 429s and the support matrix comes back partial). The operator
41	# fills/confirms the model and SIGNS. Two-family segregation vs the RUNTIME generator still holds.
42	benchmark_judge:
43	  model_slug: moonshotai/kimi-k2.6
44	  family: moonshot
45	  provider_selection: highest_operational_provider_count
46	  role_description: |
47	    Builds the binary Factual-Support Matrix for the DeepTRACE scorer (statement x listed-source).
48	    Not a runtime gate — a measurement judge. It never edits the pipeline output; it only scores it.
49	
50	# === comparability policy (BINDING) ===
51	# "#1 on DeepTRACE" is claimable ONLY if POLARIS AND every competitor are scored by the SAME
52	# benchmark judge on the SAME task set. Pick exactly ONE honest path and disclose it:
53	#   paper_judge  — use the paper's judge policy (GPT-5-class) so the number is directly comparable
54	#                  to the paper's PUBLISHED competitor scores.
55	#   self_rescore — POLARIS re-scores EVERY competitor report itself with THIS benchmark judge; a fair
56	#                  RELATIVE comparison whose ABSOLUTE numbers are disclosed as NOT the paper's numbers.
57	# NEVER compare a self_rescore POLARIS number against a paper-published competitor number.
58	comparability:
59	  policy: self_rescore
60	  competitors_rescored_with_same_judge: true
61	  absolute_numbers_disclosed_as_non_paper: true
62	  never_mix_self_and_paper_numbers: true
63	
64	# === provider / headroom preflight floor ===
65	# The statement-support call volume per report is large, so the locked judge must have real OpenRouter
66	# provider redundancy. The preflight counts OPERATIONAL endpoints for benchmark_judge.model_slug and
67	# fails loud below this floor.
68	provider_headroom:
69	  min_operational_provider_count: 8
70	  expected_statement_support_calls_per_report: 178
71	  rationale: |
72	    ~178 statement-support decisions per drb_72-class report under concurrency. Below ~8 operational
73	    providers the judge 429s and the Factual-Support Matrix returns partial -> a wrong DeepTRACE
74	    number. 8 is the measured floor at which kimi-k2.6 sustained the call burst without partials.
75	
76	# === honesty flags surfaced with every number ===
77	disclosure:
78	  is_reimplementation: true
79	  no_public_official_scorer: true
80	  judge_is_substitution_for_paper_gpt5: true
81	
82	# === operator signature (INERT until signed) ===
83	# Operator accepted in-session (voice/chat, "I accept") on 2026-07-04 UTC. The operator accepts:
84	#   (1) moonshotai/kimi-k2.6 as the benchmark-scorer judge;
85	#   (2) comparability policy = self_rescore — POLARIS re-scores EVERY competitor with THIS same judge,
86	#       and absolute numbers are disclosed as NOT the paper's published GPT-5 numbers (never mixed);
87	#   (3) disclosure = this is POLARIS's re-implementation of DeepTRACE, not the paper's official scorer.
88	# Recorded by the assistant on the operator's explicit acceptance; provider-headroom (>=8 operational
89	# providers) is still enforced at preflight time against live OpenRouter before any number is claimed.
90	signature:
91	  signed: true
92	  operator: aldrin.or@c-polarbiotech.com
93	  signed_at_utc: 2026-07-04T03:51:03Z
94	  signed_commit: 8e9811bb5c447c30303651b67b4153c38807fc19
95

Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.", "article": "# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

## Introduction and Scope

The Fourth Industrial Revolution, a term popularized by Klaus Schwab in 2016, is characterized by a fusion of technologies that blurs the lines between the physical, digital, and biological spheres, distinguishing it from previous industrial revolutions through its unprecedented velocity, scope, and systems impact.[1].[2] Artificial intelligence sits at the center of this transformation, acting as a key driver that orchestrates technologies such as robotics, automated vehicles, and real-time data analytics to redefine how work is performed across industries.[2] Global business investment in AI increased 13-fold from US$14.57 billion in 2013 to US$189 billion in 2023, with 35% of businesses already using AI in their operations and more than 50% intending to implement AI technologies.[3] This rapid diffusion has prompted predictions that STARA—smart technology, artificial intelligence, robotics, and algorithms—will replace a third of jobs that exist today, with forecasts that by 2025, 85 million jobs may be displaced while 97 million new roles may emerge that are adapted to the new division of labor between humans, machines, and algorithms.[4] Similarly, the World Economic Forum has predicted that AI adoption will make 75 million jobs redundant and create 133 million new ones worldwide by 2022, while economic analyses from PwC, IBM, Deloitte, and Gartner Research project that AI will increase global GDP by 15% by 2030, adding approximately $15.7 trillion.[5] A separate Future of Jobs report projected that 7.1 million jobs will be lost across 15 economic areas from 2015 to 2020, while 2.1 million additional jobs are expected to be created in business, financial operations, management, and computer and mathematical roles during the same period.[6] The net impact on labor demand depends on how these effects weigh against each other, and the authors find that the sharp slowdown of US wage bill growth over the last three decades is a consequence of weaker-than-usual productivity growth combined with significant shifts in the task content of production against labor, with stronger displacement effects and considerably weaker reinstatement effects in recent decades.[7] The scope of this disruption spans multiple sectors, with AI-driven automation reshaping employment through task automation, skill polarization, and industry-specific disruptions in healthcare, education, and creative industries, while also affecting white-collar professionals, gig workers, and freelancers by increasing precarity and skill mismatches.[3] Industrial robots already exceed 4 million units in operation, with installations steadily increasing due to falling costs and enhanced AI capabilities.[8] Acemoglu and Restrepo's task-based framework posits that automation generates a displacement effect, as capital takes over tasks previously performed by labor.[7] These converging trends underscore the need for a comprehensive review of AI's impact on labor markets, examining how automation displaces and reinstates labor, how industries are differentially affected, and what policy and organizational responses are emerging to address the challenges.[7][3]

## Task-Based Frameworks and Displacement-Reinstatement

In this framework, production requires the completion of a range of tasks, each of which can be allocated to either capital or labor, and the resulting allocation determines what the authors term the "task content of production".[7].[9] Countervailing the displacement effect is the reinstatement effect, which is the polar opposite of displacement and arises when new labor-intensive tasks are created in which labor has a comparative advantage, directly increasing both the labor share and labor demand.[7].[10][11] Historical examples of reinstatement abound: in the 19th century, technological developments generated employment for line workers, engineers, machinists, repairmen, conductors, managers, and financiers, while during agricultural mechanization, new occupations in factories and clerical work played a pivotal role in generating labor demand.[7] The pattern was particularly pronounced in manufacturing, where the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 1.1 percent per year, or about 30 percent cumulatively over the period.[10].[11] The authors' baseline estimate of the elasticity of substitution between capital and labor at the industry level is 0.8, based on Oberfield and Raval's (2014) work, while firm-level elasticities are estimated to be between 0.4 and 0.7.[10].[11] Complementary research by Autor, using decades of U.S. data from 1940 through 2018, estimates that more than 60 percent of employment in 2018 was found in job titles that did not exist in 1940, reinforcing the view that new work is quantitatively important.[12] Acemoglu and Restrepo's paper, published in the Journal of Economic Perspectives (DOI 10.1257/jep.33.2.3), presents a task-based framework for analyzing how technology displaces and reinstates labor.[7].[10][11][9] Automation corresponds to the development and adoption of new technologies that enable capital to be substituted for labor in a range of tasks, generating a displacement effect that shifts the task content of production adversely for labor and reduces the labor share of value added.[7].[10][11] Simultaneously, automation produces a productivity effect by increasing productivity, which contributes to the demand for labor in non-automated tasks, so that the net impact on labor demand depends on how the displacement and productivity effects weigh against each other.[7] The authors argue that some automation technologies may reduce labor demand because they bring sizable displacement effects but modest productivity gains, contradicting the presumption that all technologies increase aggregate labor demand simply because they raise productivity.[7] Using data from Lin (2011), Acemoglu and Restrepo show that about half of employment growth over the period 1980–2015 took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed.[7] Their decomposition reveals that between 1947 and 1987, wage bill per capita grew at 2.5 percent per year, largely explained by the productivity effect of 2.4 percent per year, while the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 0.48 percent per year and the reinstatement effect increased it by 0.47 percent per year, yielding a near balance.[10][11]

## Occupational Exposure and Susceptibility to AI

One such framework proposes evaluating the potential impact of large language models (LLMs) by considering their relevance to the tasks workers perform, estimating that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces and general training.[13] When accounting for current and likely future software developments that complement LLM capabilities, this share jumps to just over 46% of jobs.[13] This analysis reveals that AI's potential impact extends beyond routine tasks to include nonroutine ones such as diagnosing health conditions, programming computers, and tracking flight routes.[14] However, the study finds that some affected occupations are augmented rather than replaced—including neurologists, software engineers, and air traffic controllers—and that affected sectors such as IT, Healthcare, and Transport are experiencing labor shortages.[14] Across OECD countries, AI has made the most progress in non-routine, cognitive tasks, meaning that the occupations most exposed to AI tend to be white-collar roles such as IT professionals, business professionals, managers, and science and engineering professionals.[15] Occupations requiring manual skills and strength—such as cleaners, agricultural forestry and fishery labourers, and food preparation assistants—are the least exposed to AI.[15] Additionally, occupations with higher wages are more likely to be exposed to rapid advances in language modelling, and education and legal service sectors exhibit higher exposure.[15] In Korea, new analysis shows that more "traditional" AI appears to be associated with lower growth in full-time, permanent jobs, concentrated in the manufacturing sector, while no such association was found for generative AI.[15] The adoption of AI also appears to change the skills required of workers, as 32.2% of Korean firms reported that AI increased the kinds of skills required and 38.3% reported an increase in the level of skills required.[15] Recent approaches have replaced coarse-grained matching with more precise deep learning methods to evaluate AI's potential impact on occupational tasks.[14] In a survey of Korean firms, 95.5% reported no workforce changes at the department- or team-level following AI adoption, and 56.5% said AI had replaced specific tasks within existing jobs rather than eliminating positions.[15]

## Empirical Evidence on Employment and Labor Demand

However, in occupations where computer use is high, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure was associated with 5.7 percentage points higher employment growth over the same period.[16] Conversely, within occupations with low computer use, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure was linked to a 0.60 percentage point greater drop in usual weekly working hours, equivalent to approximately 13 minutes per week.[16] The share of job postings requiring AI skills remained very low overall, averaging 0.24% in the United States and 0.14% in the United Kingdom in 2019.[16] Instrumental variable estimates reinforced these findings, showing AI increased high-skilled employment by 0.376% and medium-skilled employment by 0.397%, while low-skilled employment declined by 0.003%.[17] Under the assumption that historical patterns of long-run substitution persist, that study estimated AI will reduce 90:10 wage inequality but will not affect the top 1%.[18] Research on generative AI's short-term effects in an online labor market, published in Organization Science, found that it reduced overall demand for workers, suggesting generative AI may diminish the role of human capital within organizations.[19] The same report projected that 30% of current U.S. jobs could be automated by 2030, with 60% of jobs seeing significant task-level modifications due to AI integration.[20] Another analysis observed that AI's impact on labor markets has been modest so far, with little evidence of broad-based job losses, echoing past innovation cycles that displaced some jobs but ultimately expanded overall employment over time.[21] One study characterized AI as having a dual purpose in the labor market, causing unemployment in routine and low-skilled occupations such as manufacturing and retail while simultaneously creating high-skilled employment in IT-related fields.[22] Across 23 countries, employment grew by 10.8% on average across all occupations and countries in the sample between 2012 and 2019.[16] A one-unit increase in AI significantly increased high-skilled employment by 0.063% and medium-skilled employment by 0.001%, while decreasing low-skilled employment by 0.001% in China.[17] Regional heterogeneity analysis across Chinese economic zones showed AI positively affected high-skilled labor employment in the Southern Coastal Economic Zone with a coefficient of 0.568 and in the Northeast Economic Zone with a coefficient of 0.211.[17] One analysis that constructed a patent-based measure of occupational AI exposure found that, in contrast to software and robots, AI is directed at high-skilled tasks.[18] A study found that AI exposure increased the relative importance of routine tasks, while robot exposure reduced the routine task share by 0.9 percentage points, accounting for virtually all of the decline in the routine task share between 2006 and 2018.[23] Jobs for AI and Machine Learning specialists have been rising at a compounding rate of 32% per year between 2016 and 2023.[22]

## Productivity Effects of Generative AI

In a preregistered online experiment, Noy et al. (2023) assigned occupation-specific writing tasks to 453 college-educated professionals and randomly exposed half to ChatGPT, finding that average time taken decreased by 40% and output quality rose by 18%.[24] The experiment also reduced inequality between workers, and those exposed to ChatGPT were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after.[24] A systematic review of 194 peer-reviewed articles published between 2011 and 2025 concludes that AI functions as a general-purpose technology capable of enhancing productivity, but its effects are often uneven and highly context-dependent, giving rise to trade-offs such as job displacement and widening inequality.[25] An ILO research brief synthesizing evidence from experiments, firm-level data, platform studies, and representative surveys across Australia, Denmark, Germany, Korea, Kuwait, the United Kingdom, and the United States finds that productivity gains are real albeit often unverified and uneven.[26] Worker-reported time savings of a few per cent of working hours have not yet translated into higher measured output, earnings, or employment.[26] Among workers who used generative AI at least once in the previous month, 31.9% spent an hour or more per workday using it, while another 47.0% used it between 15 and 59 minutes daily.[27] Users reported meaningful time savings: among those who used generative AI in the previous week, 20.5% said it saved them four hours or more, 20.1% reported three hours, 26.4% reported saving two hours, and 33.0% reported an hour or less.[27] The weighted correlation between self-reported time savings and self-reported output changes is low, suggesting that workers save time but do not on average produce more.[26] Productivity effects vary substantially by occupation: workers in the computer and mathematics occupation used generative AI in nearly 12% of their work hours and reported this saved them 2.5% of work time, while workers in personal service occupations used it in only 1.3% of their work hours, saving just 0.4%.[27] Across industries, information services has both the largest share of work hours spent using generative AI at 14.0% and the highest time savings at 2.6%, while leisure, accommodation, and other services has the lowest at 2.3% and 0.6%, respectively.[27] A 10 percentage point increase in the share of time spent using generative AI is associated with a 1.7 percentage point increase in time saved as a share of hours worked.[27] A 2024 working paper by Bonney and others found that only 5.4% of firms had formally adopted generative AI as of February 2024, suggesting that worker adoption remains mostly informal.[27] In a working paper, a standard model of aggregate production was used to estimate that self-reported time savings from generative AI translate to a 1.1% increase in aggregate productivity, implying that workers are on average 33% more productive in each hour.[27]

## Wage Inequality, Skill Demand, and Labor Polarization

Empirical evidence indicates that the share of the employed population with AI skills remains small—at most 0.3% of those employed in OECD countries on average—but is growing rapidly, with nearly half the AI workforce situated in the top two deciles of the labour earnings distribution.[28] A one standard deviation increase in exposure to AI is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, an effect largely driven by occupations requiring a high level of familiarity with software.[28].[29] Higher-income occupations exhibit a strong positive relationship between AI impact and both employment and wages, suggesting that access to complementary skills and technologies plays an important role in determining who benefits from AI adoption.[29] These patterns imply that AI has the potential to exacerbate labor market polarization, as the benefits accrue disproportionately to workers already positioned at the upper end of the skill and income distribution.[29].[30] Consistent with this concern, labor participation of low-skilled workers in the United States has declined by 2.34% and that of medium-skill workers by 2.56%, trends that one analysis links to labor-saving technologies including AI.[30] High-skilled roles such as business professionals, managers, CEOs, and science and engineering professionals are most affected by AI advances, while lower-skilled jobs experience comparatively less impact.[31] Job vacancies for AI roles have increased seven-fold since 2012, and jobs requiring AI skills have grown 3.5 times faster than overall job vacancies, signaling a steep rise in demand for specialized competencies.[31] Automation more broadly has been associated with increased wage disparities and social inequalities, as the growing disparity between automated production needs and current workforce capabilities requires coordinated efforts from industry and government to address the skills gap.[32] Theoretical perspectives on occupations and inequality identify four overarching lenses—skills, tasks, institutions, and culture—through which scholars conceptualize how occupational characteristics shape wage inequality, though some mechanisms linking occupations to wage outcomes remain empirically unclear.[33] Notably, generative AI applications have been shown to benefit the least experienced workers most, with one study finding that less experienced programmers using AI tools completed tasks over 50% faster than those in the control group, suggesting that under certain conditions AI may decrease rather than increase performance inequality in the workplace.[28] AI's impact on wages is theoretically ambiguous, with evidence indicating that positive wage effects are driven primarily by occupations with higher software skill requirements and higher-income occupations.[28].[29] Research using the AI Occupational Impact (AIOI) measure provides evidence that occupations impacted by AI experience a small but positive change in wages and no change in employment, while the positive correlation with wages is driven primarily by occupations with higher software skill requirements.[29] Surveys reveal that men—particularly in finance—were more likely than women to expect a wage increase and less likely to expect a wage decrease due to AI, indicating that AI may put further pressure on currently existing wage inequalities.[28]

## Policy Implications and Frameworks for Managing Disruption

The model features two intermediate sectors—one subject to an automation shock—to capture cross-sector labor flows and enable study of policies supporting sectoral mobility.[34] Sectoral reallocation can be costly because of potential mismatch between the skills of unemployed workers and those required by firms, drawing on Branch, Petrosky-Nadeau, and Rocheteau (2016), Di Pace and Hertweck (2019), and Walsh (2011).[34] Cazzaniga and others (2024) assume that AI shocks would reduce the labor share by 5.5 percentage points based on the historical change observed in the United Kingdom between 1980 and 2014.[34] A significantly larger share of total employment is in occupations with high augmentation potential across regions, ranging from 10.2 percent in Sub-Saharan Africa to 16.1 percent in Southeastern Asia and the Pacific.[35] Online platform workers in the US regularly report median earnings of roughly $2 to $3 per hour, well below the federal minimum wage of $7.25, and much of this low-paid work has moved to developing countries.[35]

## Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions

The cross-study evidence on AI's labor market impact reveals a field still in its earliest stages, with convergent acknowledgment that current findings are fragmented and preliminary.[36][37] The question of whether AI exposure measures are valid also produces conflicting signals: while existing measures have been criticized as poorly validated against real-world economic outcomes, Tomlinson et al. (2025) found that AI exposure measures from Eloundou et al. (2024) have very high correlation with Microsoft Copilot usage from 2025.[37] Earlier labor market disruption research, including Autor, Dorn, and Hanson's (2013) analysis of the China shock, demonstrates that disruptions can be especially challenging when geographically concentrated, with localized economic and political effects persisting across local labor markets.[36]

## Conclusions and Research Gaps

The collective evidence suggests that AI is beginning to reshape labor markets, but the research remains in an early stage with substantial unresolved questions.[37][36] Yet early large-scale evidence is consistent with the hypothesis that the AI revolution is beginning to have a significant and disproportionate impact on entry-level workers in the American labor market.[37] AI also has unclear effects on matching between employers and job candidates: algorithmic writing assistance for resumes has been shown to lead to causal increases in hiring and wages for prospective employees, while AI-assisted interviewing has been found to improve candidate selection.[37] However, AI usage may dilute labor market signals; one study finds that while AI improves cover letters, these subsequently became less informative signals of worker ability, prompting employers to shift toward alternative signals such as past reviews.[37] One analysis finds that the evidence on how AI is affecting the labor market today is inconclusive, and claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature.[36] Some research found that a decline in job postings began in 2022, prior to the public release of ChatGPT, corresponding better to the macroeconomic shift of rising interest rates than to the launch of large language models, and Frank et al. (2026) found similar results.[36] The uneven adoption of AI across regions raises critical questions about global equity, fairness, and the social contract.[35] Important questions about AI's effects on the labor market remain unanswered, and there is a need for more research and better data.[37][36][35]

## Limitations

Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 6% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 21% falling into T6, while a further 15% remain UNKNOWN and could not be reliably categorized. No contradictions were detected by the pipeline across any paired sources, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted here. The telemetry block does not surface a date range, which limits the ability to characterize the evidence horizon or identify temporal gaps in the underlying corpus.
